# Veterinarian-Client-Patient Relationship

## Veterinarian-Client-Patient Relationship

A veterinarian-client-patient relationship, or VCPR, is the first step to accessing clinical veterinary services. The VCPR represents an agreement between the client and the veterinarian.

The veterinarian-­client-­patient ­relationship supports the public’s access to safe, quality animal care. And, as the foundation of clinical veterinary medicine, the VCPR supports veterinarians in delivering effective, clinical animal care!

##### Establishing a VCPR

**In establishing a****VCPR, the veterinarian and the client have a conversation which includes****:**

**[Establishing, Maintaining and Discontinuing a VCPR](/standards/establishing,-maintaining,-and-discontinuing-a-vcpr)**

**[Guide - Establishing, Maintaining and Discontinuing a VCPR](/standards/guide-establishing,-maintaining-and-discontinuing-a-vcpr)**

##### Video: First step to accessing clinical veterinary services

 The VCPR is the foundation of effective clinical medicine and animal care.

[Checklist for establishing a VCPR](/getmedia/baf20a8d-9334-4cb5-a2d6-ca0efddcab6d/VCPR-Checklist.pdf)

##### Learning module: Establishing a VCPR

 A new learning module is available to assist you with understanding how to effectively establish a VCPR. The learning module will assist you with understanding:

[Access the learning module](/veterinary-practice/quality-practice/quality-improvement-program/learning-modules)

##### Public Expectations

When a member of the public seeks veterinary services, they expect to receive safe, ethical, quality care from a qualified professional who is licensed with the College of Veterinarians of Ontario. The College’s programs and standards support quality and safety in the delivery of veterinary medicine. Veterinarians are accountable to the College for the quality of care provided and professional conduct.

# The veterinarian-client-patient relationship (VCPR)

![VCPR](/sites/default/files/inline-images/basic/mcm-oneoffs-vcpr-website-06-2880x900-2025.jpg)

Animals enrich our lives in countless ways, and we want to keep them as happy and healthy as possible. The veterinarian-client-patient relationship, or VCPR for short, helps us do that by enabling your veterinarian to provide the best possible recommendations and medical care based on your and your animal’s needs.

### What is a veterinarian-client-patient relationship (VCPR)?

A VCPR is the professional relationship between you, your animal(s), and your veterinarian. It exists when your veterinarian knows your animal well enough—typically through a recent physical examination or regular visits—to make informed decisions about the animal’s health. This relationship is the foundation of safe, effective veterinary care. It must be in place before your veterinarian can diagnose a condition, prescribe medication, or treat your animal.

A VCPR means:

Your role in the VCPR includes:

Your veterinarian's role involves:

### How is a VCPR established and maintained?

A VCPR can only be established when your veterinarian examines your animal in person or makes medically appropriate and timely visits to the operation where animals are kept (for example, farm animals)—except in a few states where the relationship can be established electronically, such as through telemedicine. It is maintained by regular veterinary visits as needed to monitor your animal's health.

In states where the VCPR is allowed to be established electronically, an in-person examination or timely visits to your operation will still be required for the use of certain medications or for certain other activities, like issuing a health certificate or veterinary feed directive (for food-producing animals). If a VCPR is established but your veterinarian does not regularly see your animal or visit your operation afterward, the VCPR is no longer valid, and it would be unethical—and potentially illegal—for your veterinarian to dispense or prescribe medications or recommend treatment.

Once a VCPR is established, it may be possible to maintain the relationship through consultations by email, telephone, or online platforms, in between medically necessary, hands-on exams or visits. Your veterinarian will use their professional judgment to determine whether this would be in your animal’s best interests.

### Why is the VCPR so important?

The VCPR supports the needs of animals, animal owners, and veterinarians alike. It fosters trust and communication, empowering you to make informed decisions and support your animal’s long-term health. What’s more, when your veterinarian knows your animal’s health history and current condition—usually through regular, in-person exams or visits, they can more accurately diagnose issues, make sound medical judgments, and provide care and recommendations tailored to your animal’s individual needs.

There also are legal aspects. Most states require that a VCPR be in place for your veterinarian to diagnose or treat your animal, or prescribe or dispense medications. In addition, federal law requires that a VCPR exist before a veterinarian can prescribe certain medications or issue health certificates or veterinary feed directives. In those cases, the VCPR must have been established in person, whether through a hands-on physical examination or medically appropriate and timely visits to the operation where the animal is kept—with no exceptions.

### Why is a hands-on physical examination so important?

Unlike people, animals can’t tell us what’s wrong, and figuring this out is like solving a very complex puzzle. A comprehensive, hands-on physical examination and other diagnostic tests allow your veterinarian to gather all the puzzle pieces and fit them together to see the full picture. With that picture, your veterinarian is better able to accurately determine your animal’s health status and recommend the best treatment. This can't be achieved through a telephone, email, or online conversation. Without a hands-on examination, there’s a greater risk of misdiagnosis, inappropriate or ineffective treatment, and other potentially harmful outcomes.

### When does a VCPR end?

Your VCPR will automatically end if you allow the regular veterinary visits recommended by your veterinarian to lapse. (Some states dictate by law a maximum interval between in-person examinations.) You, as the client, also can intentionally terminate your VCPR at any time by notifying your veterinarian.

If your veterinarian chooses to end your VCPR, they should notify you and, if your animal has an immediate, life-threatening illness, provide medical care until you have transitioned to another veterinarian.

enriched_points.expanded_content: The VCPR is a formal agreement between a veterinarian, the client, and the patient, ensuring informed veterinary care and communication tailored to the animal's needs.
The VCPR is crucial for telemedicine as it establishes trust and ensures veterinarians can provide safe, effective care remotely, based on the animal's health history.
Legal frameworks mandate that a VCPR exists before veterinarians can diagnose, treat, or prescribe medications, ensuring ethical and responsible veterinary practice.
